1: ʾāhavtî kî-yišmaʿ yhwh ʾet-qôlî taḥănûnāy — "I love, because the LORD hears my voice, my supplications" — uniquely, ʾāhavtî (I love) has no stated object in Hebrew; the object of love is implied from the whole psalm. The verb is Qal perfect, a settled state: "I am in a state of love." taḥănûnāy (my supplications, cries for mercy, from ḥānan, to be gracious) is plural, suggesting repeated urgent requests over time, not a single prayer.
2: kî-hiṭṭāh ʾoznô lî ûbĕyāmay ʾeqrāʾ — "because he has bent his ear to me; so for all my days I will call on him" — hiṭṭāh ʾoznô (inclined his ear, Hiphil of nāṭāh) — a vivid anthropomorphism: God tilts his ear downward to catch the prayer from below. The practical consequence is lifelong prayer: the experience of being heard generates a permanent posture of calling.
3: ʾăpāpûnî ḥeblê-māwet ûmĕṣārê šĕʾôl mĕṣāʾûnî — "the ropes/cords of death surrounded me; the constrictions of Sheol found me" — ḥeblê-māwet (ropes/cords of death) — ḥebel means "cord/rope" and (with different vowels) "sorrow/pain"; the LXX reads ōdines (birth pangs). Either way the image is entrapment. mĕṣārê šĕʾôl (constrictions/straits of Sheol, from ṣārar, to bind, constrict) — Sheol closes in like a vise. ṣārāh wĕyāgôn ʾemṣāʾ — "distress and anguish I found."
4: ûbĕšēm-yhwh ʾeqrāʾ ʾānnāh yhwh mallĕṭāh napšî — "then I called on the name of the LORD: 'O LORD, please — rescue my life!'" — ʾānnāh (please, I beseech you) is the interjection of urgent petition (also Psalms 118:25; Jonah 1:14). mallĕṭāh napšî (rescue/deliver my life, Piel imperative of mālaṭ) — Piel is intensive: "snatch me out, pull me free." The entire saving event hinges on this single cry.
5: ḥannûn yhwh wĕṣaddîq wĕʾĕlōhênû mĕraḥēm — "gracious is the LORD and righteous; our God is compassionate" — the same triad as Exodus 34:6 and Psalms 111:4 (ḥannûn, ṣaddîq, and raḥûm). In context the declaration is the psalmist's testimony after being heard: the rescue reveals what kind of God YHWH is. mĕraḥēm (showing compassion, Piel participle of rāḥam) derives from reḥem (womb) — divine compassion is womb-deep.
6: šōmēr pĕtāʾyim yhwh dallōtî wĕlî yĕhôšîaʿ — "the LORD preserves the simple (pĕtāʾyim); I was brought low, and he saved me" — pĕtāʾyim (simple, naive, from pāṭāh, to be open/gullible) — the Proverbs vocabulary for those without experience. The LXX renders nēpia (infants, little ones). The "simple" need a guardian, not because they are foolish, but because they are inexperienced and open. dallōtî (I was brought low, Qal perfect of dālal, to hang low, be reduced) — he made no claim of merit; he simply fell, and God caught him.
7: šûbî napšî limnûḥāyĕkî kî-yhwh gāmal ʿālāyĕkî — "return, O my soul, to your rest, for the LORD has been generous to you" — the psalmist speaks to his own nepeš (soul) — an interior dialogue rare in the Psalter (cf. Psalms 42:5, 11; Psalms 103:1). limnûḥāyĕkî (to your resting place, from nûaḥ, to rest) — the same root as Noah's name. gāmal (to deal fully, to recompense, to be generous) — often used of both reward and punishment; here the generosity is the rescue just completed.
8: kî ḥillaṣtā napšî mimmāwet ʾet-ʿênî min-dimʿāh ʾet-raglî middĕḥî — "for you have rescued my life from death, my eye from tears, my foot from stumbling" — ḥillaṣ (to draw out, rescue, Piel of ḥālaṣ) — originally to pull off a sandal, extended to extricating from any trap. The triple object (life from death; eye from tears; foot from stumbling) covers the whole person: existential, emotional, practical. The three rescue clauses mirror the three death-forms of v3.
9: ʾethallēk lipnê yhwh bĕʾarṣôt haḥayyîm — "I will walk before the LORD in the land of the living" — ʾethallēk (Hithpael of hālak, to walk about, walk back and forth) — the Hithpael suggests ongoing, sustained walking, not a single stride. lipnê yhwh (before the LORD, in his presence) — covenant language for the life lived in YHWH's sight (Genesis 17:1: "walk before me and be blameless"). bĕʾarṣôt haḥayyîm (in the land of the living) — the world of the living as opposed to Sheol.
10: heʾĕmantî kî ʾădabbēr — "I believed/trusted, because I spoke" (or "I trusted, therefore I spoke") — the syntax is famously ambiguous: does kî introduce a result clause or a causal one? The LXX reads it as "I believed, therefore I spoke" (episteusan, dio elalēsa), which is the version Paul cites in 2 Corinthians 4:13 for his own apostolic speech: "Since we have the same spirit of faith according to what has been written, 'I believed, and so I spoke,' we also believe, and so we also speak." heʾĕmantî (Hiphil perfect of ʾāman) = the root of ʾāmēn.
11: ʾănî ʾāmartî bĕḥopsî kol-hāʾādām kōzēb — "I said in my panic/haste, 'All men are liars/faithless'" — bĕḥopsî (in my alarm, haste, panic, from ḥāpaz, to be startled, flee in panic) — the same root as the sea's "flight" described in Psalms 114:5. The panic-declaration that all humans are unreliable (kōzēb, a liar) is an extreme expression from within crisis — not a settled philosophical conviction. Paul quotes this in Romans 3:4 ("let God be true and every man a liar"), demonstrating how the same verse can be deployed in different theological contexts.
12: māh-ʾāšîb layhwāh kol-tagmûlôhî ʿālay — "what shall I return/give back to the LORD for all his benefits toward me?" — māh-ʾāšîb (what shall I return?) — šûb (return, restore) frames the gift-and-return dynamic of covenant life: YHWH has given; the psalmist asks what return is appropriate. tagmûlôhî (his benefits, his full dealings, from gāmal) — the same root as v7's generosity; the psalmist is overwhelmed by the magnitude of the gift.
13: kôs-yĕshûʿôt ʾeśśāʾ ûbĕšēm yhwh ʾeqrāʾ — "I will lift up the cup of salvations and call on the name of the LORD" — kôs-yĕshûʿôt (cup of salvations) — the plural yĕshûʿôt (salvations, rescues) is significant: not a single rescue but multiple or great salvations. Ellicott identifies the kôs with the drink offering (nesek) accompanying festival sacrifices (Numbers 15:5-10). The psalmist's return to God is not payment but further worship. The NT Last Supper's "cup of blessing" (1 Corinthians 10:16) stands in this tradition.
14: nĕdāray layhwāh ʾăšallēm neged-nāʾ lĕkhol-ʿammô — "my vows to the LORD I will pay, now in the presence of all his people" — nĕdāray (my vows, from nādar, to vow) — the psalmist had made vows during the crisis (v4: "I called on the name of the LORD"); now he fulfills them publicly. The public fulfillment of vows is liturgically essential: the congregation witnesses and validates the testimony. This verse repeats verbatim as v18, forming a refrain around the central v15.
15: yāqār bĕʿênê yhwh hammāwĕtāh laḥăsîdāyw — "precious/costly in the eyes of the LORD is the death of his faithful ones (ḥăsîdîm)" — the most theologically debated verse in the psalm. yāqār (precious, costly, rare, heavy) — the ambiguity is productive: (1) the death of his faithful is valued by God (making martyrdom meritorious); (2) the death of his faithful is costly to God (he grieves it, takes it seriously). Most modern scholars and the context favor (2): God does not regard the death of his ḥăsîdîm lightly — he takes it seriously, which is why he rescues (vv3-8) and why the vow-fulfillment matters (vv13-18). The ḥăsîdîm are the covenant people at their most devoted.
16: ʾānnāh yhwh kî-ʾănî ʿabdĕkā ʾănî-ʿabdĕkā ben-ʾămātĕkā pittaḥtā lĕmôsērāy — "O LORD, I am your servant — your servant, son of your handmaid; you have loosed my bonds" — ben-ʾămātĕkā (son of your handmaid/female servant) — a son born in the owner's household, a hereditary servant from birth rather than one acquired. This heightens the covenantal intimacy. Psalms 86:16 uses the identical phrase. pittaḥtā lĕmôsērāy — "you have opened/loosed my bonds/fetters" — pātaḥ (to open, loosen) applied to mōsērôt (bonds, fetters) picks up the ropes of death from v3.
17: lĕkā ʾezbaḥ zebaḥ tôdāh ûbĕšēm yhwh ʾeqrāʾ — "to you I will offer a sacrifice of thanksgiving (tôdāh) and call on the name of the LORD" — the zebaḥ tôdāh (thanksgiving sacrifice) was a specific cultic category in Leviticus 7:11-15: a peace offering made when someone had been delivered from danger (illness, sea crossing, prison, desert). Ps 116 is itself a tôdāh psalm — both the literary form (testimony of rescue followed by praise) and the cultic act it likely accompanied.
18: nĕdāray layhwāh ʾăšallēm neged-nāʾ lĕkhol-ʿammô — verbatim repetition of v14, forming the refrain that frames v15. The public fulfillment of vows (v14 → v15 → v18) moves from obligation to declaration of God's seriousness about his servants' deaths to completion. The structural repetition signals liturgical performance: the congregation hears the vow-fulfillment formula twice, around the theologically central v15.
19: bĕḥaṣrôt bêt-yhwh bĕtôkēkî yĕrûšālāim hallĕlûyāh — "in the courtyards of the house of the LORD, in your midst, O Jerusalem. Praise the LORD!" — the psalm closes with geographic specificity: the vow is fulfilled not just before "all his people" (vv14,18) but in the temple courts, in Jerusalem. This is the pilgrimage psalm's destination — the pilgrim arrives and fulfills his vow. hallĕlûyāh closes the psalm and opens directly into Ps 117, the briefest and most universal of the Hallel psalms.
